category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Partial Differential Equations/Green Functions and Boundary Value Problems

questiontext.text: The Green's function \(G(x,y)\) for the operator \(L = -\Delta\) on \(\Omega\) with Dirichlet BCs satisfies:

feedback.text: Correct. The Green's function is the fundamental solution corrected to satisfy zero boundary conditions. Given G, the solution to −Δu=f with u=0 on ∂Ω is u(x)=∫_Ω G(x,y)f(y)dy.
This reverses the roles: the delta source is in the interior, not on the boundary. The boundary condition is G=0 (Dirichlet), not G=δ.
A unit source everywhere corresponds to solving −Δu=1 (Poisson equation), not the Green's function which uses a point source δ(x−y).
The fundamental solution Φ does not vanish on ∂Ω in general. The Green's function G=Φ−h^y where h^y is harmonic and chosen so G=0 on ∂Ω.

questiontext.text: The Green's function for the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain \(\Omega\) satisfies:

feedback.text: Correct. The Green's function of a self-adjoint operator (like −Δ with Dirichlet BCs) is symmetric: G(x,y)=G(y,x). This follows from Green's second identity: ∫(G(x,·)ΔG(y,·)−G(y,·)ΔG(x,·))=0 gives G(x,y)=G(y,x).
The Green's function of a self-adjoint elliptic operator is symmetric, not antisymmetric. G(x,y)=G(y,x).

The Green's function IS non-negative for the Dirichlet Laplacian on bounded domains (by the maximum principle). While this is a correct statement, the specific symmetry property G(x,y)=G(y,x) is the key property asked about.
Translation invariance holds only when Ω=ℝⁿ (or a half-space/whole space). For general bounded domains, G depends on both x and y separately, not just x+y.

questiontext.text: The Neumann Green's function (also called the Neumann function) \(N(x,y)\) for \(-\Delta\) on \(\Omega\) satisfies:

feedback.text: Correct. For the Neumann problem, the compatibility condition requires a correction: the source is δ(x−y)−1/|Ω| (average subtracted to ensure the Neumann compatibility condition ∫(RHS)=0). The Neumann function is unique up to a constant.
This is the Dirichlet Green's function, not the Neumann function. For Neumann BCs, the normal derivative of N vanishes on ∂Ω.
Without the compatibility correction, −ΔN=δ(x−y) with Neumann BCs has no solution (since ∫δ(x−y)dx=1≠0=∫(∂N/∂ν)dS by the divergence theorem). The correct equation has δ(x−y)−1/|Ω| on the right.

answer.text: \(N(x,y) = G(x,y)\) (Dirichlet and Neumann Green's functions are the same)

feedback.text: The Dirichlet and Neumann Green's functions are different objects (different boundary conditions). They satisfy different PDEs on the boundary.

questiontext.text: The retarded Green's function for the 1D wave equation \(u_{tt} - c^2 u_{xx} = f(x,t)\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The retarded Green's function for the 1D wave operator is (1/2c) times the indicator of the forward light cone |x−ξ|<c(t−τ). It satisfies □G=δ(x−ξ)δ(t−τ) with G=0 for t<τ. The solution by Duhamel is u=∫∫G f dξ dτ.

This is the 1D Green's function expressed as a delta function on the light cone. In 1D this equals (1/2c)·1_{|x−ξ|=c(t−τ)}, which is the derivative of the retarded Green's function (related to the wave front, not the full cone).
This is the 3D retarded Green's function (Kirchhoff kernel), not the 1D one. In 1D, G is supported on the interior of the light cone (not just the surface).
This is the heat kernel, not the wave equation Green's function. The wave equation Green's function has compact support on the light cone, not a Gaussian.

questiontext.text: For the Sturm-Liouville problem \(Lu = -(pu')' + qu = f\) on \([a,b]\) with Dirichlet BCs, the Green's function \(G(x,y)\) is:

answer.text: \(G(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{W}\begin{cases} u_1(x)\,u_2(y) & x \leq y \\ u_1(y)\,u_2(x) & x \geq y\end{cases}\), where \(u_1,u_2\) satisfy the BCs at \(a,b\) respectively and \(W = p(u_1'u_2 - u_1u_2')\).

feedback.text: Correct. The Green's function for a Sturm-Liouville BVP is built from two solutions u₁ (satisfying the left BC) and u₂ (satisfying the right BC), normalized by the Wronskian W. The jump condition from the delta source is incorporated in this formula.
Using u₁u₂ for all x,y without the piecewise structure violates the required symmetry G(x,y)=G(y,x) and does not satisfy the delta-source equation.
The spectral expansion G(x,y)=Σφₙ(x)φₙ(y)/λₙ is valid when all eigenvalues λₙ>0 and gives the same Green's function. But the piecewise formula is the direct construction using fundamental solutions.
The translation-invariant fundamental solution applies to constant coefficient operators on ℝⁿ. Sturm-Liouville operators have variable coefficients, requiring the piecewise construction.

questiontext.text: The solution of \(-\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), can be written in terms of eigenfunctions \(\phi_n\) of \(-\Delta\) as:

feedback.text: Correct. Expanding u = Σaₙφₙ and f = Σfₙφₙ in eigenfunctions, then −Δu = Σaₙλₙφₙ = Σfₙφₙ implies aₙ=fₙ/λₙ. This is valid since λₙ>0 for the Dirichlet Laplacian.
Inverted: the correct formula divides by λₙ (the eigenvalue), not multiplies. Dividing corresponds to inverting the operator −Δ.
This would give u=f, which satisfies −Δu=−Δf≠f in general. The eigenfunction expansion of the SOLUTION requires dividing by eigenvalues.
Dividing by λₙ² would arise from solving (−Δ)²u=f (biharmonic), not −Δu=f. The Laplacian requires division by λₙ, not λₙ².

questiontext.text: The Robin (or mixed) boundary condition for the Poisson equation \(-\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. Robin BCs interpolate between Dirichlet (β=0) and Neumann (α=0). They arise naturally in heat transfer (Newton's cooling law: heat flux proportional to temperature difference). For α,β>0, the problem has a unique solution.

answer.text: \(u = 0\) on part of \(\partial\Omega\) and \(\partial u/\partial\nu = 0\) on the rest (mixed Dirichlet-Neumann)

feedback.text: This is a mixed Dirichlet-Neumann condition (different BCs on different parts of ∂Ω), not the Robin condition (which is a linear combination αu+β∂u/∂ν on the same boundary portion).
This is not a standard boundary condition for elliptic PDEs. Boundary conditions specify u or ∂u/∂ν (or linear combinations) on ∂Ω, not values of Δu there.
This is a periodic boundary condition, used for torus domains. The Robin condition is αu+β∂u/∂ν=g, not a periodicity requirement.

questiontext.text: The Green's function for the heat equation \(u_t - k\Delta u = f\) on \(\Omega \times (0,T)\) with \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) and \(u(x,0)=0\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The heat kernel on Ω with Dirichlet BCs expands in eigenfunctions: G(x,t;ξ,τ) = Σ_n e^{−kλₙ(t−τ)} φₙ(x)φₙ(ξ) for t>τ, using the eigenvalues/eigenfunctions of −Δ with Dirichlet BCs.
This is the free-space heat kernel on ℝⁿ. For bounded Ω with Dirichlet BCs, the Green's function must vanish on ∂Ω and involves the eigenfunction expansion, not the full-space Gaussian.
Using only the first eigenmode is not the complete Green's function. All modes contribute: G = Σₙ e^{−kλₙ(t−τ)}φₙ(x)φₙ(ξ).

answer.text: \(G(x,t;\xi,\tau) = G(x,t-\tau;\xi,0)\) (time-translation invariant only, no spatial component)

feedback.text: While G is time-translation invariant (depends on t−τ, not t and τ separately), this statement alone does not specify what G is. The complete formula involves the eigenfunction expansion.

questiontext.text: For the Dirichlet Laplacian \(-\Delta\) on a bounded domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\), the spectral theorem asserts:

answer.text: The eigenvalues \(0 < \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq \cdots \to \infty\) are positive and the eigenfunctions \(\phi_n\) form a complete orthonormal basis for \(L^2(\Omega)\).

feedback.text: Correct. The Dirichlet Laplacian is a positive self-adjoint operator with compact resolvent on bounded Ω. Its spectrum consists of positive eigenvalues accumulating at +∞, and the eigenfunctions form a complete ONB for L²(Ω).

answer.text: The eigenvalues can be negative, zero, or positive depending on \(\Omega\).

feedback.text: For the Dirichlet Laplacian (with Dirichlet BCs u=0 on ∂Ω), all eigenvalues are strictly positive. This follows from the energy identity ∫|∇φ|² = λ∫φ² (so λ=‖∇φ‖²/‖φ‖²>0 for φ≢0).

answer.text: There are only finitely many eigenvalues for bounded \(\Omega\).

feedback.text: There are infinitely many eigenvalues, accumulating at +∞. The operator has compact resolvent, which implies a discrete (not finite) spectrum.

answer.text: The eigenfunctions are polynomials restricted to \(\Omega\).

feedback.text: Eigenfunctions are not polynomials in general; they satisfy −Δφ=λφ with Dirichlet BCs. For simple domains (rectangles, balls, spheres), they are products of trigonometric/Bessel functions, not polynomials.

questiontext.text: Weyl's law describes the asymptotic distribution of eigenvalues of \(-\Delta\) on a bounded domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\): the counting function \(N(\lambda) = \#\{n : \lambda_n \leq \lambda\}\) satisfies:

feedback.text: Correct. Weyl's law: N(λ) ~ (ωₙ/(2π)ⁿ)|Ω|λ^{n/2} where ωₙ is the volume of the unit ball in ℝⁿ. Equivalently, λₙ ~ (2π)²(n/ωₙ|Ω|)^{2/n}·n^{2/n}. This shows eigenvalue density depends on the volume of Ω.
The eigenvalue counting function grows polynomially (λ^{n/2}), not exponentially. Exponential growth would occur for infinite-dimensional problems, not the finite-volume Laplacian.
Linear growth N~Cλ is Weyl's law only in n=2. In general dimension n, N(λ)~Cλ^{n/2}. For n=1: N~Cλ^{1/2}, for n=3: N~Cλ^{3/2}.
The leading term in Weyl's law depends on the volume |Ω|, not the perimeter. Perimeter (surface area) appears in the second term of the Weyl expansion (Weyl conjecture, now a theorem).

questiontext.text: The solution of \(-\Delta u = 0\) in \(\Omega\), \(u = g\) on \(\partial\Omega\) via the Green's function is:

feedback.text: Correct. For the Laplace equation with Dirichlet boundary data g, Green's formula gives u(x) = −∮_{∂Ω} g(y)(∂G/∂ν_y)(x,y)dS_y. The normal derivative of G (Poisson kernel) acts as the reproducing kernel for boundary data.
G(x,y)=0 for y∈∂Ω (Dirichlet Green's function), so this integral is identically 0. The correct formula uses the normal derivative ∂G/∂ν_y (Poisson kernel), not G itself.
Volume integrals with G give solutions when there is a volume source. For boundary data with no interior source, the formula uses the boundary integral with ∂G/∂ν.
g is defined on ∂Ω (boundary data), not on Ω. The solution u is the harmonic extension of g into the interior, computed via the Poisson kernel integral.

questiontext.text: The Poisson kernel for the upper half-space \(\mathbb{R}^n_+ = \{x : x_n > 0\}\) (solving \(\Delta u=0\) with \(u(x',0)=f(x')\)) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The Poisson kernel for the upper half-space is P(x',x_n;y') = 2x_n/(ωₙ(|x'−y'|²+x_n²)^{n/2}). In 3D: P = x₃/(2π(|x'−y'|²+x₃²)^{3/2}). This kernel satisfies ΔP=0, P→0 as x_n→∞, and P→δ(x'−y') as x_n→0⁺.
The solution to Laplace's equation cannot be trivially f(x') in the interior (that would mean u has no xₙ-dependence, violating harmonicity unless f is harmonic itself).
e^{−xₙ}f(x') satisfies u_{x_nx_n}=e^{−xₙ}f(x'), which combines with the x' Laplacian term to give Δu=e^{−xₙ}(f−Δ_{x'}f)≠0 unless f is harmonic. This is not the Poisson formula.
This is the heat kernel convolution with time = xₙ, solving the heat equation (not Laplace's equation). The Poisson formula for the half-space uses the algebraic Poisson kernel, not the Gaussian.

questiontext.text: For the Dirichlet problem \(-\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u=g\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the Lax-Milgram theorem guarantees a unique weak solution \(u \in H^1_0(\Omega)\) when:

answer.text: \(f \in H^{-1}(\Omega)\) (the dual of \(H^1_0(\Omega)\)) and \(g = 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) (or \(g \in H^{1/2}(\partial\Omega)\) in general).

feedback.text: Correct. The bilinear form a(u,v)=∫∇u·∇v dx is coercive and continuous on H¹₀(Ω) by Poincaré's inequality. Lax-Milgram then gives a unique u∈H¹₀ for each f∈H⁻¹. Non-zero Dirichlet data requires lifting to homogeneous BCs.
Classical continuity is sufficient for the classical solution (using maximum principle and Schauder estimates), but Lax-Milgram works in the Sobolev space framework with f∈H⁻¹, broader than C(Ω).

answer.text: \(\Omega\) must be convex for the Lax-Milgram theorem to apply.

feedback.text: Lax-Milgram applies to any bounded Lipschitz domain Ω. Convexity gives additional regularity (u∈H²) but is not required for existence of the weak solution.

answer.text: The theorem fails for \(-\Delta\) (it only applies to first-order operators).

feedback.text: Lax-Milgram applies to any bounded, coercive bilinear form on a Hilbert space. The bilinear form for −Δ (Dirichlet problem) is a(u,v)=∫∇u·∇v, which is coercive by Poincaré's inequality.

questiontext.text: The double layer potential \(u(x) = \displaystyle\oint_{\partial\Omega} \dfrac{\partial\Phi}{\partial\nu_y}(x-y)\,\phi(y)\,dS_y\) (for \(x \in \Omega\)) satisfies:

feedback.text: Correct. Both single and double layer potentials are harmonic in the complement of the boundary ∂Ω (since Φ(x−y) is harmonic in x for x≠y). They are used in boundary integral equation methods to solve Laplace's equation.
The layer potential is harmonic in the INTERIOR (and exterior) of Ω. The source density φ lives on ∂Ω and the jump conditions relate the boundary values of u to φ.
Layer potentials are harmonic (Δu=0), not uniformly sourced (Δu=1). Newton potentials (volume integrals with Φ) produce Poisson equations.

answer.text: The double layer potential is not harmonic; it satisfies the heat equation.

feedback.text: The double layer potential for Laplace's equation is harmonic (Δu=0) in the interior and exterior of ∂Ω. Heat equation solutions require a heat kernel, not the Laplace fundamental solution.

questiontext.text: The jump condition for the double layer potential \(u(x) = \displaystyle\oint_{\partial\Omega} \dfrac{\partial\Phi(x-y)}{\partial\nu_y}\phi(y)\,dS_y\) as \(x \to x_0 \in \partial\Omega\) from inside \(\Omega\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The double layer potential has a jump of ±φ/2 across ∂Ω. The limiting value from inside ("+") is u⁺(x₀) = φ(x₀)/2 + P.V.∮(∂Φ/∂ν_y)φ dS. From outside: u⁻(x₀) = −φ(x₀)/2 + P.V.∮(∂Φ/∂ν_y)φ dS.

answer.text: \(u^+(x_0) = u^-(x_0)\) (no jump — the double layer potential is continuous)

feedback.text: The double layer potential has a jump discontinuity of φ(x₀) across ∂Ω. It is the normal derivative of the single layer potential that may be continuous (or have a jump).
The limit equals (1/2)φ(x₀) plus the principal value integral, not just φ(x₀) alone.
The double layer potential does not vanish on ∂Ω in general; it has a well-defined (but jumpy) boundary trace involving φ and a principal value integral.

questiontext.text: Seeking a solution of the interior Dirichlet problem as a double layer potential \(u = K\phi\) leads to the Fredholm integral equation of the second kind:

feedback.text: Correct. Applying the boundary data condition u⁺=g and using the jump relation gives the Fredholm second kind integral equation (I/2 + K)φ = g where K is the double layer operator. This can be solved by Fredholm theory (compact perturbation of identity).
First kind equations (no φ(x) outside the integral) are typically ill-posed. The double layer approach gives a second kind equation with the (1/2)φ(x) term from the jump condition.
The density φ is not equal to the boundary data g directly. The integral equation relating φ and g must be solved to find φ, then u is reconstructed from φ.
The density φ lives on ∂Ω (a 1-codimensional manifold) and satisfies an integral equation, not a PDE in Ω.

questiontext.text: The capacity (or logarithmic capacity in 2D) of a compact set \(K \subset \mathbb{R}^n\) is related to the Green's function \(G_K\) by:

answer.text: The equilibrium measure on \(K\) minimizes the energy \(\displaystyle\iint \log\dfrac{1}{|x-y|}d\mu(x)d\mu(y)\) over probability measures supported on \(K\).

feedback.text: Correct. The logarithmic capacity in 2D is defined via the equilibrium problem: the capacity C(K)=exp(−min energy) where the energy is the logarithmic energy. In higher dimensions, the Riesz s-energy and Newton capacity play analogous roles.

answer.text: The capacity equals the volume \(|K|\) of the compact set.

feedback.text: Capacity is a potential-theoretic concept, not the Lebesgue measure. A set can have zero Lebesgue measure but positive capacity (e.g., a smooth hypersurface in ℝⁿ).

answer.text: Sets of zero capacity can support Dirichlet boundary conditions for the Laplacian.

feedback.text: Sets of zero capacity are "negligible" for the Laplacian — the Dirichlet problem cannot use them as boundary since solutions cannot detect such sets. Only sets of positive capacity can carry meaningful boundary conditions.

answer.text: The capacity of a ball \(B(0,r)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) is proportional to \(r^2\).

feedback.text: The Newtonian capacity of B(0,r) in ℝ³ is proportional to r (not r²). The capacity of a ball scales like r^{n−2} in ℝⁿ for n≥3.

questiontext.text: A boundary point \(x_0 \in \partial\Omega\) is called regular (for the Dirichlet problem) if:

answer.text: The Perron solution \(u\) satisfies \(u(x) \to g(x_0)\) as \(x \to x_0\) from inside \(\Omega\), for every continuous boundary data \(g\).

feedback.text: Correct. A boundary point is regular if the Perron solution takes the prescribed boundary value there. Regular points are characterized by the existence of a barrier (a local superharmonic function vanishing at x₀). Irregular points arise at cusps, cracks, or isolated points (Lebesgue's thorn example).
C¹ smooth boundary points are regular, but regularity is a weaker condition. For example, points on a Lipschitz boundary (not necessarily C¹) are also regular.
Regularity is a concept for BOUNDARY points x₀∈∂Ω. Interior points always satisfy the Perron property by the mean value theorem.
The fundamental solution Φ(x−y)→∞ as x→y, but this is a property of the source point (interior), not the boundary regularity. Boundary regularity concerns the behavior of harmonic functions near ∂Ω.

questiontext.text: Poincaré's inequality states: for \(u \in H^1_0(\Omega)\) (bounded \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\)):

answer.text: \(\|u\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \leq C_P\|\nabla u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\), where \(C_P\) depends only on \(\Omega\).

feedback.text: Correct. Poincaré's inequality: for functions vanishing on ∂Ω, the L² norm is controlled by the gradient norm. C_P = 1/√λ₁ where λ₁ is the first Dirichlet eigenvalue. This is crucial for the coercivity of the bilinear form in Lax-Milgram.
This is the reverse direction — not Poincaré's inequality. In general, ‖∇u‖ can be much larger than ‖u‖ (consider high-frequency oscillations).
This is an elliptic regularity estimate, not Poincaré's inequality. Poincaré's inequality relates ‖u‖_{L²} to ‖∇u‖_{L²} for functions with zero boundary values.
Poincaré's inequality holds in all dimensions n≥1 for bounded domains and functions in H¹₀(Ω). It is one of the fundamental inequalities in Sobolev space theory.

questiontext.text: The Sobolev embedding theorem states: for \(u \in H^k(\Omega)\) with \(k > n/2\) (\(n\) = dimension of \(\Omega\)):

answer.text: \(u \in C(\overline{\Omega})\) (continuous), and the embedding \(H^k \hookrightarrow C^{k-\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1}(\overline{\Omega})\) is compact.

feedback.text: Correct. When k>n/2, H^k functions have enough Sobolev regularity to be continuous (and more). Specifically H^k↪C^{k−⌊n/2⌋−1} (Morrey's inequality). For n=1: H¹↪C⁰ (k>1/2 so k=1 gives continuity). For n=3: H²↪C⁰ (k>3/2 so k=2 gives continuity).

answer.text: \(u \in L^\infty(\Omega)\) only (bounded but not necessarily continuous).

feedback.text: When k>n/2, H^k functions are not just L^∞ but actually continuous (and Hölder). L^∞ alone would correspond to weaker conditions.
Sobolev embeddings relate H^k to C^m or L^p spaces (not to higher Sobolev spaces). Moving between H^k spaces of different orders requires elliptic regularity, not just the embedding theorem.

answer.text: The embedding fails for \(k > n/2\); higher \(k\) gives worse regularity.

feedback.text: Higher k gives BETTER regularity via Sobolev embedding. The critical threshold is k = n/2: below it H^k need not be continuous; above it H^k embeds into C^{k−⌊n/2⌋−1}.
